You can definitely distinguish CLI execution without extra variables.
The Flask CLI automatically sets the `FLASK_APP` environment variable.
You might check if the command name exists in the system arguments.
A simple way is to inspect the `sys.argv` list directly.
If the second argument matches your specific command name, you know.
This approach avoids the need for manual environment variable setting.
You could also create a custom `create_app` function for CLI use.
The `create_app` function can accept a boolean flag for context.
Passing a flag like `is_cli=True` helps differentiate the mode.
However, the CLI loader does not pass arguments to `create_app` directly.
You must parse the arguments inside the application factory itself.
Reading `sys.argv` inside `create_app` is a valid and clean solution.
This allows you to detect the command before the app fully initializes.
You can then configure your app differently based on that detection.
This method ensures your server and CLI logic remain separate.
You do not need to remember to type extra variables every time.
The solution relies on standard Python libraries and Flask internals.
It is robust enough for most production and development scenarios.
You might also consider using a custom click group for your commands.
This gives you full control over how the app is initialized.
By defining the CLI commands manually, you bypass the default loader.
This manual definition allows you to pass custom parameters easily.
You can now run `flask my_command` without any extra flags.
Your code will automatically detect the context and act accordingly.
